mechanism: Measure subjective valuations of objects across two experimental conditions: isolated evaluation and joint evaluation. For each condition, record preference judgments (rankings or ratings) on a common scale. Compare the direction and magnitude of preference reversal between conditions. The analysis tests whether attribute-weighting during evaluation differs systematically when options are presented separately versus together, hypothesizing that isolated evaluation emphasizes easily-evaluable but less important attributes while joint evaluation redirects attention to more important attributes.

Obtain affective ratings (emotional valence) and analytic risk/benefit judgments for hazards in two experimental contexts: normal deliberation and time-pressure conditions. Compute correlation or regression between affect and each judgment dimension across both conditions. Test whether time pressure increases the strength of inverse risk-benefit association and whether manipulating affect of a known item systematically shifts both risk and benefit estimates in the same direction. The hypothesis posits affect as a common latent variable generating correlated outputs.

Stage 1: two subjects independently encode an observation. Stage 2: both discuss the observation and record agreement/disagreement. Stage 3: both independently retrieve the observation and report details. For discussed items, quantify whether subjects report information matching their discussion partner's account despite internal knowledge differing. For non-discussed items, quantify accuracy without partner influence. Compare accuracy and conformity rates between discussed and non-discussed conditions to isolate the conformity effect from general forgetting.

Partition subjects into groups by belief profile (paranormal, religious, skeptic). Present images with and without face-like regions. Measure detection accuracy (hits and false alarms) and ratings of face-likeness. Compute sensitivity (d') and response bias (criterion) via signal detection theory. Test whether belief groups differ in d' (actual perceptual sensitivity) versus criterion (decision threshold). Compare face-likeness and emotionality ratings between groups.

Manipulate victim presentation (single vs. group; identified vs. non-identified) in a between-subjects design. Measure helping behavior (monetary contribution) and self-reported emotional distress. Compare mean contributions and distress ratings across the four conditions. Test the interaction between victim singularity and identification status. Decompose the overall 'identified victim effect' into components attributable to singularity versus information availability.

Present numerical estimation tasks with incidental environmental cues (jersey number, product model number, business name number) and measure numeric estimates. Compare mean estimates in conditions where anchor is high versus low. Test whether estimation differs by domain expertise or whether subjects recall the anchor. The core computation is a simple comparison of estimate distributions conditional on anchor presence and magnitude.

Two samples (US and Hong Kong) judge spatial placement of entities (hypothetical people of high/low socioeconomic status) on a north-south axis or report personal residence preference along the same axis. Test whether cardinal direction (north/south) predicts valence-related placement or preference within each sample and compare the direction of association between samples. Measure correlation or odds ratios of high-valence-to-north assignment in each cultural context.

Subjects rank letters of the alphabet by preference and own-name letters are identified. Count how many own-name letters appear in each subject's top-6 preferred letters and top-6 disliked letters. Compare the distribution of own-name frequencies in high-preference versus low-preference positions. Aggregate across 12 language groups. Test whether own-name status predicts higher preference rank controlling for letter identity.
domain: preference and ownership effects, cross-linguistic cognition

mechanism: Study 1: extract newspaper mentions of an athlete's nationality from an archival database, coded by performance outcome (win/loss). Count nationality assignments in each condition and test association. Study 2: manipulate athlete's dual identity (shared vs. non-shared with in-group) and performance outcome in a factorial design. Measure perceived nationality and similarity ratings. Test whether nationality classification is more favorable to in-group when outcome is positive, dependent on identity overlap.

Assign entities (rats) to three groups: delay-exposure training, immediacy-exposure training, and no-training control. During training, entities choose between small immediate and large delayed rewards. After training, measure impulsive choice frequency (preference for small immediate reward) in a choice task. Compare the pre-to-post change in impulsive choice between groups via within-group and between-group contrasts. Test whether delay-exposure reduces impulsive choice relative to controls or increases immediate-reward preferences.

Conduct a verbal preference assessment to classify visual stimuli into high-preference and low-preference categories for each entity. Then expose the entity to all stimulus categories and measure response force (via dynamometer) for each. Compute mean response force per stimulus category. Test whether high-preference stimulus categories correlate with higher mean response force than low-preference categories.

Design a multi-item self-report questionnaire organized into five subscales measuring intergenerational workplace climate. Collect responses on a Likert scale. Compute subscale scores and their internal consistency (reliability). In Study 1, correlate subscale scores with external outcomes (mentoring, job satisfaction). In Study 2, fit a structural equation model to validate subscale structure and test relationships. In Study 3, assess criterion validity in a diverse sample via regression and moderation analysis.

Randomly assign subjects to receive low or high expectation statements about a government service and expose them to visual information depicting low or high service performance. Measure satisfaction judgments on a scale. Compute mean satisfaction in each of the four conditions. Test main effects of expectation and performance and their interaction. The effect magnitude of expectation-disconfirmation is estimated via regression with performance controlling for baseline expectation.

Measure five personality dimensions (via questionnaire) and three body weight measures (BMI, waist circumference, waist-to-hip ratio) at two time points separated by ~4 years. Fit cross-sectional regression models at each timepoint with personality predicting body weight, controlling for demographic variables. Fit longitudinal models with baseline personality predicting weight-change between timepoints. Test for gender-specific associations via interaction terms. Estimate personality coefficients separately for male and female subsamples.

Collect scores on narcissism pathways (agentic and antagonistic dimensions) for multiple samples. Fit latent class analysis (mixture model) to classify subjects into unobserved subgroups. Extract class membership probabilities. Test whether the same latent classes emerge across independent samples (Germany and US). Relate class membership to external variables (personality, adjustment outcomes) and test group differences in those outcomes via one-way ANOVA or regression.

Classify subjects by alexithymia score (high vs. low). Present words (emotion-related) during encoding with congruent or incongruent background music (happy/angry). Measure recognition accuracy for each word type in each condition. Compute hit and false-alarm rates and proportion correct. Test main effects of alexithymia level and music-word congruency on recognition and their interaction via ANOVA.

Collect paired observations of 85 couples at baseline and 3-month follow-up. Measure optimism, perceived social support from recipient and provider perspectives, and stress. Fit Actor-Partner Interdependence Models that allow one member's optimism to predict their own stress (actor effect) and the partner's stress (partner effect). Test whether perceived support mediates the optimism-to-stress relationship via conditional indirect effects. Compare mediation paths for recipient-perspective versus provider-perspective support.

Survey firms in emerging economies to measure perceived informal competition and new product development activity. Fit logistic regression with informal competition predicting NPD propensity (binary outcome). Test moderation of this relationship by formal competition level, corruption availability, and managerial regulatory optimism. Estimate direct effects and conditional indirect effects (moderated path). Data come from approximately 9000 observations across regions.

Assign subjects (balanced by gender) to a dictator game with either 'give' frame (choose amount to give to recipient) or 'take' frame (choose amount to take from recipient), with identical payoff matrices. Measure allocation (amount transferred) by gender and frame. Compute means and test main effects of gender and frame and their interaction. Test whether gender effects reverse or reverse between frames and whether giving equals 'not taking' in the aggregate.

Design a laboratory commons dilemma with two nested decisions: investment in shared infrastructure and extraction from a common-pool resource. Vary the asymmetry of payoffs or resource availability across subjects. Implement communication (vs. no-communication) condition. Measure resource provision, extraction rates, and group coordination success. Test whether communication improves coordination and whether asymmetry impairs or maintains cooperation. Compare outcomes between treatment conditions.
